content.heading: The JS → Rust mental shift
content.lead: You already think like a programmer. This guide rewires that thinking for Rust ; using JavaScript and TypeScript as the translation layer throughout.

sections.text: Why Rust feels strange at first
Every unusual piece of Rust syntax is solving a problem JavaScript handles invisibly. Once you see what it's solving, the syntax makes sense on its own.

rows: Also builds, runs, tests ; one tool

sections.text: Four things that make Rust different
1. Compiled, not interpreted. Your code becomes native machine code before it ever runs. Many JS runtime errors simply cannot exist in compiled Rust.
2. Static types ; no surprises. Every value has a type the compiler knows at compile time. No "5" + 5 = "55" surprises.
3. No garbage collector. JavaScript's GC cleans up memory in the background. Rust uses ownership rules to free memory exactly when needed ; zero runtime overhead.
4. Immutable by default. In JS, let is mutable. In Rust, let is immutable. You opt into mutation with let mut.

sections.head: Key mindset
sections.text: When Rust rejects your code, it's not being difficult ; it's catching a bug you'd spend hours debugging at runtime. The compiler is your first line of defence. Its error messages often suggest exactly what to fix.

content.heading: Setup & cargo
content.lead: Installing Rust and learning cargo ; which is npm, webpack, and jest rolled into one binary.

sections.text: Installing Rust
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
This installs: rustc (the compiler), cargo (build + package manager), and rustup (toolchain manager). Verify:

rustc --version   # e.g. rustc 1.78.0
cargo --version   # e.g. cargo 1.78.0
cargo ≈ npm (but more)
Create the project

cargo new vaultlog
cd vaultlog
This scaffolds:

vaultlog/
├── Cargo.toml     # like package.json
└── src/
    └── main.rs    # entry point ; like index.js
Cargo.toml ; your package.json

sections.head: Features in Cargo.toml
sections.text: Some crates have optional features. serde = { features = ["derive"] } unlocks #[derive(Serialize)] on your structs. Think of it like opting into optional peer dependencies ; you only pay for what you use.
Modules ; like JS files
vaultlog splits across multiple files. Each file is a module. You declare them in main.rs:

mod models;   // loads src/models.rs
mod ledger;   // loads src/ledger.rs
mod storage;  // loads src/storage.rs

content.heading: Variables & types
content.lead: Rust's type system is the foundation. Stricter than TypeScript, more explicit ; but that strictness is what makes Rust safe and fast.

sections.text: let is immutable by default

left.label: JavaScript

right.label: Rust

sections.head: JS translation
sections.text: Think of Rust's let as JS's const. Think of Rust's let mut as JS's let.
Numeric types
JS has one number type. Rust has many ; it cares exactly how much memory a value takes and whether it can be negative:
The two string types ; this really matters

rows: Borrowed reference to a string. Fixed-size. Usually string literals.
Function params, strings you only read
Owned, heap-allocated string. Can grow, can be modified.
Struct fields, strings built at runtime

right.label: Rust ; two string types

sections.head: Rule of thumb
sections.text: Use String for struct fields. Use &str for function parameters where you only need to read. Convert: "hello".to_string() gives a String; &my_string gives a &str.

content.heading: Structs & enums
content.lead: These are how you model your data. Structs are TypeScript interfaces with methods. Rust enums are far more powerful than anything TypeScript has.

sections.text: Structs ; typed data containers

left.label: TypeScript interface

right.label: Rust struct
right.code:
pub struct LogEntry {
    pub id: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub category: EntryCategory,
    pub previous_hash: String,
    pub hash: String,
}

sections.text: pub is like export in JS ; everything in Rust is private by default.
The #[derive] attribute ; auto-generated code

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LogEntry { /* ... */ }

sections.head: Traits
sections.text: A trait is Rust's version of an interface ; it defines behaviour a type can have. When you #[derive(...)] one, Rust writes the implementation for you. When you need custom behaviour, you write it in an impl block.
Enums ; sum types with real power

left.label: TypeScript enum (just constants)

right.label: Rust enum (variants)

sections.text: Enums with data ; where it gets interesting
Each variant can hold completely different data. This is how the CLI Commands enum works:
Option<T> ; Rust's null replacement
Rust has no null. Instead, a built-in enum:

content.heading: Functions & methods
content.lead: How to write functions, attach behaviour to structs with impl blocks, and understand Rust's implicit-return rule.

sections.text: Defining functions

left.label: TypeScript

right.label: Rust

sections.head: Semicolons are semantic
sections.text: In Rust, a semicolon turns an expression (which has a value) into a statement (which doesn't). The last line with no semicolon is the implicit return. Add a semicolon and you'll get: expected String, found (). The fix: remove the semicolon.
impl blocks ; attaching methods to structs
In JS, methods live inside classes. In Rust, data (struct) and behaviour (impl) are deliberately separate:

left.label: JavaScript class

right.label: Rust struct + impl

sections.text: Three flavors of self
compute_hash ; the real vaultlog function
.expect("message") is the quick way to crash with a message if something fails. In production you'd use ? instead (chapter 5).

content.heading: Error handling
content.lead: One of the biggest mental shifts from JS. Rust has no exceptions ; errors are values your functions return explicitly.

sections.text: Result<T, E> ; Rust's try/catch replacement
Result is a built-in enum with two variants:

left.label: JavaScript try/catch

right.label: Rust Result

sections.text: The ? operator ; error propagation made easy
The ? at the end of a line is the most important operator for beginners to learn. It means: "if this returns Err, immediately return that error from my function. If it's Ok, unwrap and continue."
Box<dyn Error> ; accepting any error type
In vaultlog's storage functions you'll see this return type:

rows: Propagate errors up ; use this in almost every function
Panic with message ; fine in prototypes, avoid in libraries
Panic silently ; only when you're certain it's Ok
When you need to handle Ok and Err differently

sections.head: The () unit type
sections.text: () pronounced "unit" is Rust's "no meaningful value" type. Result<(), E> means "succeeds with nothing, or fails with an error" ; like a void function that can fail.

content.heading: Ownership & borrowing
content.lead: The concept unique to Rust. There is no garbage collector ; ownership rules determine exactly when memory is freed. This is what the borrow checker enforces.

sections.text: Three ownership rules

items: Every value in Rust has exactly one owner.
There can only ever be one owner at a time.
When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (freed).

sections.head: Why JS never does this
sections.text: In JS, objects are reference-counted by the GC ; multiple variables can "point" to the same data. Rust forbids this to eliminate entire classes of bugs (data races, use-after-free). The trade-off: you think about ownership explicitly.
Borrowing with & ; read without owning
Instead of moving, you can lend a value temporarily:
Borrowing rules

rows: Exactly one &mut T at a time
One writer ; no readers while writing
No reading while something is being mutated

sections.text: Clone ; explicit deep copy
In vaultlog, #[derive(Clone)] on LogEntry lets you call .clone() on any entry whenever you need a second independent copy.

sections.head: When you hit borrow checker errors
sections.text: Read the error message carefully ; Rust's compiler tells you exactly what the problem is and often suggests the fix. The most common fix is either: pass a reference (&entry instead of entry), or clone when you genuinely need two owners (entry.clone()).

content.heading: Iterators & closures
content.lead: Rust's iterator methods look a lot like JavaScript's array methods ; .map(), .filter(), .for_each(). The main difference: they're lazy and need .collect().

sections.text: Closures ; like arrow functions

left.label: JavaScript

right.label: Rust

sections.text: Iterators are lazy ; collect() runs them
In JS, .map() runs immediately. In Rust, iterator chains are lazy ; they build up a pipeline and do nothing until you call .collect():
storage.rs ; reading entries with iterators
filter_map is like .filter().map() combined ; it keeps only elements where the closure returns Some(value) and discards None. It's your go-to for fallible transformations.
Common iterator methods

content.heading: Pattern matching
content.lead: match is Rust's supercharged switch statement. It's exhaustive ; the compiler forces you to handle every possible case. You'll use it constantly.

sections.text: Basic match

left.label: JavaScript switch

right.label: Rust match

sections.text: Matching enums with data ; the CLI dispatcher
This is the heart of vaultlog's main.rs. Each Commands variant holds different data, and match extracts it:
if let ; matching a single variant
When you only care about one case, if let is cleaner than a full match:
The Clap CLI derive pattern
Clap reads those #[derive] and #[arg] annotations and generates a full CLI parser. The triple-slash comments (///) become help text.

content.heading: Putting it all together
content.lead: Here's the full vaultlog data flow from CLI input to verified ledger ; every concept from every chapter in one picture.

sections.text: Complete main.rs
How it maps to REAL (your bigger project)
Day 5 polish checklist

items: Add the colored crate for terminal colours ; cargo add colored
Handle the "file not found" case in read_all_entries gracefully (return empty vec)
Write a solid README explaining the hash chain concept
Push to GitHub ; this is real work worth showing
Spend an hour writing down every Rust concept you used and how it maps to REAL

sections.head: You're not starting over when you build REAL
sections.text: Every pattern in vaultlog scales directly. The struct becomes a richer model. The flat file becomes a database. The CLI becomes an Axum REST API. The hash chain stays identical. You're expanding what you built ; not rewriting it.
